interest: Teacher unions/labor and equity advocates (general-track teachers, Roma-rights and disadvantaged-parent groups) resisting a policy that keeps early sorting intact while loading compensatory and remedial workload onto the schools least resourced to bear it.
value: Structural equity and durable reform versus administrative continuity and cosmetic compensation—dismantling the sorting engine versus managing its downstream effects indefinitely.
fear: That compensation becomes a permanent substitute for reform: general-school teachers absorb indefinite, under-resourced high-complexity workload while sorting is legitimized as forever, and politically fragile TEIP-style funding evaporates in the first budget cut, leaving burden without resources.
affected: parents of pupils sorted out at age 10
assumption: That legitimizing compensation removes the political pressure needed for structural reform, and that compensation can at best mask rather than offset early-sorting effects—so S4 permanently entrenches selection rather than acting as a bridge to reform.
empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: the scenario itself states no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age (10-12) and the Portuguese TEIP precedent did not involve early tracking, so whether compensation offsets or merely masks stratification cannot be determined from available evidence; the budget-vulnerability claim rests on the scenario's own 'first line item cut' flag.
